sourceTitle: Assign custom database permissions to make your life easier

questions.question: Which permission level stops users from editing a database’s structure while still allowing them to modify the content inside database pages?

options.text: Full access
options.explanation: Full access lets users change both structure and content.

options.text: Can edit content
options.explanation: The “Can edit content” role blocks structural changes but permits editing page content.

options.text: Can view
options.explanation: Can view only allows reading, not editing any content.

options.text: Can edit structure
options.explanation: There is no such built‑in role; it would do the opposite of what’s described.

questions.question: A user with “Can edit content” permission wants a personal view of tasks assigned to them without altering the main database. How can they achieve this?

options.text: Create a new view directly in the main database
options.explanation: Users with this role cannot create or modify views in the main database.

options.text: Add a linked database block on another page and build the view there
options.explanation: Linked databases let them create custom views that don’t affect the source database.

options.text: Change the database’s name to include their name
options.explanation: Renaming the database is a structural change, which is blocked.

options.text: Use the @me filter in the main database’s existing view
options.explanation: While @me works, it still edits the shared view; the question asks for a personal view without affecting the main database.

questions.question: When a row in a Notion database matches multiple page‑level permission rules, which rule determines the user’s access?

options.text: The rule that was created first
options.explanation: Creation order does not affect precedence.

options.text: The rule with the most specific property
options.explanation: Specificity is not used; Notion chooses the highest level of access.

options.text: The rule that grants the highest level of access
options.explanation: If multiple rules apply, Notion gives the user the most permissive (highest) access.

options.text: The rule assigned to the user’s role group
options.explanation: Role groups are just another rule; the highest‑access rule still wins.

questions.question: Which of the following actions is **not** allowed for a user with the “Can edit content” role?

options.text: Creating and deleting database entries (pages)
options.explanation: Content editors can add or remove rows.

options.text: Editing properties in a given row
options.explanation: Editing row‑level properties is permitted.

options.text: Changing a property’s type (e.g., from text to number)
options.explanation: Changing property types is a structural edit, which is blocked for this role.

options.text: Using an existing database template
options.explanation: Templates can be used by content editors.

questions.question: In a large editorial calendar, what is the recommended permission setup for most writers?

options.text: Full access so they can adjust the calendar layout as needed
options.explanation: Full access gives too much control over structure.

options.text: “Can edit content” role, while a few admins retain full access
options.explanation: Writers get enough rights to add content without risking structural changes; admins keep full access.

options.text: Read‑only access, letting them only view the calendar
options.explanation: Read‑only would prevent them from adding or updating articles.

options.text: Custom role that allows adding new select options
options.explanation: Allowing new select values defeats the purpose of protecting data definitions.

questions.question: Why does preventing users with “Can edit content” from adding new select or multi‑select values help keep data definitions sacred?

options.text: It forces all users to use the same color scheme for tags
options.explanation: Color schemes are unrelated to data definition integrity.

options.text: It stops accidental creation of duplicate or misspelled status values that could break downstream workflows
options.explanation: New values can cause inconsistencies and downstream errors; blocking them preserves a stable taxonomy.

options.text: It makes the database load faster by limiting the number of options
options.explanation: Performance impact is negligible; the main goal is data consistency.

options.text: It allows administrators to hide select fields from view
options.explanation: Hiding fields is a separate permission; the restriction is about adding values, not visibility.

questions.question: Where do you locate the “Can edit content” option when assigning permissions to a Notion database?

options.text: In the database’s Properties panel
options.explanation: Properties panel manages fields, not permissions.

options.text: In the Share menu dropdown of the database page
options.explanation: The Share menu contains the permission level selector, including “Can edit content”.

options.text: Under Settings → Advanced → Permissions
options.explanation: Notion does not have this navigation path for database permissions.

options.text: When creating a new database template
options.explanation: Templates are for content, not for setting permission levels.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using page‑level permissions on individual database rows?

options.text: It automatically syncs the row to all linked databases
options.explanation: Syncing is unrelated to page‑level access.

options.text: It lets you control who can view, edit, or comment on that specific row, independent of database‑wide access
options.explanation: Page‑level permissions give fine‑grained control over each row’s visibility and edit rights.

options.text: It changes the row’s property types based on the viewer
options.explanation: Property types are structural and cannot be altered per viewer.

options.text: It converts the row into a separate database with its own permissions
options.explanation: Rows remain part of the same database; they just have their own Share settings.
